methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $15M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 4 same-sector + same-stage peers in AI & Software. Range: $250M - $400.0B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in AI & Compute

private.name: Palantir
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $400B+

private.name: Liquid AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2.35B

private.name: Field AI
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $2B

private.name: Twelve Labs
private.rationale: Same sector (AI & Software), same stage (Series A), post-money $250M

data.narrative:
Atmo is a Series A-stage AI & Software company founded by Alex Levy and Johan Mathe that has raised $15M+ to date. The valuation analysis triangulates across a comparable universe of 9 peers (1 public, 8 private) in the same sector and stage, including Palantir, Liquid AI, Field AI, and Twelve Labs.

Two methods anchor the valuation. The stage-progression approach applies a 2.0x multiplier to the $15M raised as a value proxy, implying $30M. The peer-median method references four same-sector, same-stage comparables with a median valuation of $2.2B, though the range is wide: $250M (Twelve Labs) to $400B (Palantir). The peer-median carries significant noise given the heterogeneity in this segment.

The triangulated analysis yields a low estimate of $26M, median of $1.1B, and high of $2.5B. The stage-progression method produces a conservative floor, while the peer median is heavily influenced by outliers—particularly Palantir's $400B valuation, which skews the upper bound. Key uncertainties include the relative maturity and revenue traction of Atmo versus peers, the timing and size of Series B demand, and whether the peer set adequately reflects Atmo's specific market positioning within AI & Software. The wide dispersion ($26M–$2.5B) reflects genuine ambiguity in early-stage AI valuations rather than analytical precision.
